local_names.romanized_local: Bò hé níng méng shuǐ (Mandarin romanization)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lemonade entry (FOODON_03301710); mint-lemonade variant is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lemonade and similar lemon-flavoured soft drinks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers lemonade-type beverages; mint variant not separately enumerated. Closest parent code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for mint lemonade beverage; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of lemonade mint (approximately 250 ml / 250 g) as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre or café.

nutrient_source.title: Lemonade Mint — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Lemonade Mint (cr_id G00011028). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mint lemonade in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 67 (medium) by analogy with sugar-sweetened lemonade / fruit-flavoured sugar drinks (GI ~65–70) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (67) × available carbohydrate per serving (9.02 g/100g × 250 g serving = 22.55 g) ÷ 100 = 15.1, rounded to 15.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.71 g sugar per 100 g, exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; negligible potassium (8 mg/100 g) and zero dietary fibre provide no compensatory cardiovascular benefit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened lemonade entirely; the high sugar load promotes insulin resistance and weight gain, both of which elevate blood pressure, and the negligible potassium content offers no vasodilatory offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rapidly absorbed liquid sugar (8.71 g/100 g, GI ~67, GL ~15 per 250 ml serving) with zero dietary fibre causes acute postprandial glucose spikes; classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA guidelines.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage; the combination of high sugar content, medium-to-high GI, and absence of fibre produces rapid glycaemic excursions that are incompatible with blood glucose management targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added-sugar content (8.71 g/100 g) in a liquid form promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and contributes to excess caloric intake; fat and saturated fat are zero, which is neutral, but the sugar burden is the primary CVD concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened lemonade, as regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased risk of coronary heart disease and dyslipidaemia per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 8.71 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre and no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols above threshold, fibre >3 g) provides no positive RA benefit; sugar promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sweetened lemonade; the high sugar content promotes inflammatory mediators, and an unsweetened lemon-mint water would be a preferable alternative with minor polyphenol benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemonade Mint (薄荷柠檬水) is a chilled sugar-sweetened beverage widely served at Singapore hawker centres, cafés, and food courts. It is prepared by combining freshly squeezed or concentrated lemon juice with sugar syrup, fresh mint leaves, and chilled water, then blending or stirring until the mint is well incorporated. The drink is characterised by a bright, tart citrus flavour balanced by the cool, herbal freshness of mint and a pronounced sweetness from added sugar. Per 100 g, it provides 93 kcal, 9.02 g carbohydrate (of which 8.71 g is sugar), negligible protein (0 g) and fat (0 g), and 5 mg sodium. Potassium is low at 8 mg/100 g and dietary fibre is absent. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both ADA and AHA guidelines due to its sugar content exceeding 5 g/100 g. It is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease because the rapidly absorbed liquid sugar causes acute postprandial glucose spikes, promotes hypertriglyceridaemia, and contributes to excess caloric intake without compensatory fibre or micronutrient benefit. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 67 (medium) by analogy with similar sugar-sweetened lemon drinks. A healthier alternative is unsweetened lemon water with fresh mint, which retains the flavour profile without the glycaemic burden.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lemonade mint?
Is mint lemonade suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of lemonade mint?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lemonade Mint (cr_id G00011028) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary regional approved source for Singapore dishes.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero as this is a sugar-water-based beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero by formulation — lemon juice, mint, sugar, and water contain no fat. saturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and dietary_fibre_g are confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this beverage and are not available in the Singapore HPB database for this specific item as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~67, medium) based on sugar-sweetened lemonade data from Atkinson et al. 2008 / University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mint lemonade exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = 67 × (9.02 × 250/100) ÷ 100 = 15.2. Q8 fix (iteration 3): the tamil alias 'எலுமிச்சை புதினா பானம்' in aliases_by_language was verified to match the flat aliases array exactly — the previous iteration contained a mixed-script corruption ('எலுமிச்சை புதina பானம்') in aliases_by_language which has been corrected to full Tamil script 'எலுமிச்சை புதினா பானம்' to ensure verbatim consistency with the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft created from HPB Foodo source data.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; added missing romanized alias 'Bò hé níng méng shuǐ' to flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Fixed Q8 failure — corrected mixed-script corruption in aliases_by_language tamil array from 'எலுமிச்சை புதina பானம்' (with embedded Latin characters) to full Tamil script 'எலுமிச்சை புதினா பானம்', ensuring verbatim consistency with the flat aliases array. All 14 flat alias entries verified against all aliases_by_language values.
